factual-accuracy.requirement: States that in a 2019 cross-sectional survey of Rohingya refugee women in Camp-4, Cox’s Bazar, approximately 71.6% of respondents reported attending at least four antenatal care visits (ANC4+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that in Cox’s Bazar “some reports indicate 71.6% to 80.5% of Rohingya women complete four or more ANC visits,” which includes the specific 71.6% ANC4+ figure for Rohingya women in the camp setting.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that in a retrospective analysis of Thailand–Myanmar border camp maternal health data through 2018 (including Mae La), more than 90% of pregnant women attended ≥4 antenatal care visits (ANC4+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states under the Mae La Camp ANC4+ section that “ANC completion rates are consistently high, with over 90% of pregnant women regularly attending four or more visits,” referencing retrospective data through 2018 (including Mae La).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya facility births/SBA started at 12-22% in 2018 (or as low as 4% in late 2017 emergency period)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that in Cox’s Bazar “facility births increased from around 22% in 2018,” which matches the required 12–22% starting level for 2018. It does not mention the alternative “as low as 4% in late 2017 emergency period,” but the 2018 22% figure satisfies the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States at least three distinct year–percentage pairs documenting facility-based delivery among Rohingya refugees in Cox’s Bazar, including one estimate ≤15% in 2018, one intermediate estimate between 40–60% from 2019–2020, and one estimate ≥75% from 2022–2023
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives facility-based delivery estimates for Cox’s Bazar of “around 22% in 2018” and “over 80%…by 2023,” but it does not provide an intermediate 2019–2020 estimate between 40–60%. It also lacks any 2018 estimate at or below 15%. Therefore it does not include the required three year–percentage pairs meeting the specified thresholds.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La facility births/SBA at 75-80% with stable mature service delivery
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Mae La Camp skilled birth attendance coverage averages 78–80% and describes it as maintained for many years due to integrated, culturally sensitive services, which corresponds to stable mature service delivery.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya NMR as 27 per 1,000 live births for September 2017-December 2018 period with most deaths occurring at home
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that neonatal mortality in Cox’s Bazar is approximately 27 per 1,000 live births and adds that most neonatal deaths happen outside facilities (i.e., at home), aligning with the specified September 2017–December 2018 period context.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La NMR declined to approximately 10-11 per 1,000 live births by 2011 and remained stable
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Mae La neonatal mortality declined from 21.8 to 10.7 per 1,000 live births between 2008 and 2011, which matches the approximate 10–11 by 2011 requirement and implies stability at that level. It does not explicitly say it “remained stable” after 2011.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya MMR approximately 200-230 per 100,000 live births in 2022-2023 period (accounting for methodology variation between 200 and 295)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives Rohingya MMR as “estimated between 295 to 446 per 100,000 live births” and later mentions ~446 by early 2024. It does not state an approximate 200–230 per 100,000 for the 2022–2023 period, nor does it frame 200–295 as a methodology-variation range for that period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that the maternal mortality ratio among Rohingya women in Cox’s Bazar refugee camps was reported at 295 per 100,000 live births in 2023 according to health sector reporting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Rohingya maternal mortality ratio in Cox’s Bazar was “estimated between 295 to 446” and does not specify that 295 per 100,000 live births was the 2023 figure. It also does not attribute the 295 value to 2023 health sector reporting.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La or SMRU Thai-resident refugee cohorts had low MMR (~79-126 per 100,000) with zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Mae La Camp MMR is ~126.5 per 100,000 live births, but it does not mention the Mae La or SMRU Thai-resident refugee cohorts having low MMR in the ~79–126 range nor does it state there have been zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005. The required “zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005” element is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that obstetric hemorrhage (including postpartum hemorrhage) is reported as a leading direct cause category of maternal deaths in the Rohingya Cox’s Bazar MPMSR surveillance reports, and states that postpartum hemorrhage contributes to maternal deaths in Thailand–Myanmar border refugee/migrant settings
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that in Cox’s Bazar “obstetric hemorrhage (~29%)” is a primary cause and that “PPH is the leading cause of maternal deaths,” referencing MPMSR reports. It does not explicitly state that postpartum hemorrhage contributes to maternal deaths in Thailand–Myanmar border refugee/migrant settings, only noting scarce incidence data and implying better outcomes.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States routine PPH incidence per 1,000 births is not publicly reported or available for either setting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says that for Mae La camp, “Specific PPH incidence data are scarce,” but it does not state that routine PPH incidence per 1,000 births is not publicly reported or available for either setting. It also discusses PPH in Cox’s Bazar without noting routine incidence unavailability there.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that health care in the Cox’s Bazar Rohingya camps is at least 2 of the following: delivered through a coordinated humanitarian partner system (WHO-led Health Sector with NGO/UN partners), includes primary health care centers, includes field hospitals, or explicitly notes the presence of 24/7 PHCC services.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Cox’s Bazar healthcare as dominated by multiple NGOs operating facilities (including field hospitals) and explicitly mentions comprehensive emergency obstetric services with “24/7” capability. This satisfies at least two required elements: inclusion of field hospitals and explicit 24/7 services.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Mae La as NGO primary care (SMRU, PU-AMI, or Malteser) integrated with Thai Ministry of Public Health for referrals
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Mae La’s services as comprehensive primary health care delivered through NGO coordination (naming SMRU and Malteser) and explicitly states they are integrated into Thailand’s Ministry of Public Health system with facilitated referrals to Thai government hospitals.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Cox's Bazar has >400 midwives active in response or approximately 213 midwives at UNFPA facilities during 2021-2022
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses midwife turnover and low midwife-to-population ratios in Cox’s Bazar but does not state that Cox’s Bazar has >400 midwives active in the response or approximately 213 midwives at UNFPA facilities during 2021–2022. Neither of the required midwife-count figures appears anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States at least one of the following: that no published peer-reviewed/authoritative reports provide a midwife-to-population ratio for Cox’s Bazar refugee camps or host communities, OR explicitly avoids reporting a numeric ratio for midwives in this setting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the midwife-to-population ratio qualitatively (e.g., “low ratio” and “below ideal standards”) without giving any numeric midwife-to-population ratio for Cox’s Bazar or host communities. This explicitly avoids reporting a numeric ratio in that setting, satisfying the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La has approximately 20 camp-resident SBAs or describes small stable established SBA workforce mentored by SMRU
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Mae La’s midwife workforce as “more stable” and “locally trained cadres,” but it does not state that Mae La has approximately 20 camp-resident SBAs or explicitly describe a small, stable, established SBA workforce mentored by SMRU. SMRU is mentioned only as a coordinating NGO without linking it to mentoring a small SBA cadre.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Cox's Bazar EmONC access including on-camp Friendship CEmONC hospital and UNFPA referral hubs launched 2019-2020 providing 24/7 transport
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “NGO-operated CEmONC facilities” and “24/7 transport via Referral Hubs,” but it does not specifically describe Cox’s Bazar EmONC access as including an on-camp Friendship CEmONC hospital, nor does it state that UNFPA referral hubs were launched in 2019–2020. Those named elements and dates are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La requires referral to Thai MoPH hospitals for C-sections at distance of 60-80 km or typically under 2 hours travel
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that Mae La has emergency obstetric care accessible within a two-hour radius and referrals to Thai government hospitals, but it does not state that C-sections specifically require referral to Thai MoPH hospitals at a distance of 60–80 km or typically under 2 hours. The required distance range (60–80 km) is not mentioned.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TBAs in both settings transitioned from delivery providers to mediators, companions, or community mobilizers rather than clinical attendants
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses TBAs being trained to promote referrals and facility births and being integrated into health promotion, but it does not state that TBAs in both settings transitioned from delivery providers to mediators/companions/community mobilizers rather than clinical attendants. It lacks an explicit comparison showing this role shift occurred in both Cox’s Bazar and Mae La.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes 2021 UNFPA funding impact was mixed or limited evidence with services continuing to improve, and more acute disruptions documented in 2023-2025 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the 2021 UNFPA funding reduction as causing service strain and disruptions in both Cox’s Bazar and Mae La while noting services continued (e.g., MSF deliveries, Thai MoPH stepping in). It also references more acute disruptions in later years (e.g., 2024–2025 funding cuts/aid freeze), aligning with the criterion’s “mixed/limited evidence in 2021” and “more acute disruptions in 2023–2025” framing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Shows at least 3 intermediate time points for key indicator progression (e.g., facility births 32%→52.7%→60%→82%) not just baseline and endpoint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides only one clear multi-point progression for a key indicator: skilled birth attendance/facility births in Cox’s Bazar (around 22% in 2018 to over 80% by 2023). Other indicators (ANC 4+, neonatal mortality, MMR, PPH) are given as ranges or single time points without at least three intermediate time points.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly explains how specific inputs (midwife scale-up, referral hubs) drove outcome changes rather than merely correlating them
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response goes beyond correlation by attributing changes to mechanisms such as NGO-led referral hubs improving timely EmOC access, midwife training/scale-up increasing skilled birth attendance, and TBA cultural mediation increasing facility births. It also links UNFPA funding cuts to reduced midwife retention and mobile team functionality, explaining resulting service continuity impacts.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Addresses apparent contradictions in source data and explains why different figures appear (methodology, scope, or temporal differences)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes conflicting ANC figures in Cox’s Bazar (e.g., 71.6–80.5% completing 4+ visits vs ~47% with no ANC) and attributes differences to disparities across surveys/coverage and barriers, implying scope/population differences. However, it does not systematically reconcile other differing figures (e.g., time periods for neonatal mortality, MMR estimates) with explicit methodology/temporal explanations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Clearly separates 2017-2023 data from post-period updates and flags when cited figures fall outside requested timeframe
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes multiple post-2023 figures and events (e.g., “early 2024” MMR, 2025 aid freeze, 2025/2026 updates) mixed into the main analysis without clearly separating them from 2017–2023 results. It does not consistently flag these as outside the requested timeframe or isolate them in a distinct post-period updates section.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains how specific delivery model features (parallel vs integrated, CEmONC location, referral design) produce different outcome patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links delivery-model features to outcomes by contrasting Cox’s Bazar’s fragmented NGO field-hospital system versus Mae La’s integrated Thai MoPH model, and explaining how referral/EmOC within 2 hours and workforce stability relate to ANC/SBA and mortality patterns. It also discusses cultural mediation via TBA training/recognition and how these design choices affect facility birth uptake and PPH/neonatal outcomes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies EmONC signal functions available, facility counts/types, or referral pathway details beyond general model description
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response goes beyond general model description by describing emergency obstetric care access within a two-hour radius, mentioning referral hubs and facilitated referrals to hospitals, and explicitly referencing CEmONC facilities and 24/7 transport constraints.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Questions or qualifies the 2021 funding reduction claim with evidence rather than accepting premise, noting what data support or contradict it
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response treats the “2021 UNFPA funding reduction” as a given and asserts impacts (e.g., workforce decline, service closures) without questioning the premise or citing specific UNHCR HIS/MSF data that would substantiate or contradict the claimed reduction. It does not qualify the claim by discussing what evidence supports it versus alternative explanations or uncertainties.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Treats Cox's Bazar parallel humanitarian system and Mae La integrated Thai MoPH model as functionally equivalent without distinguishing their structural differences
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts Cox’s Bazar as a fragmented, NGO-operated field-hospital system with Mae La as integrated into Thailand’s Ministry of Public Health, noting differences in coordination, workforce stability, referral pathways, and cultural mediation. It does not present the two models as functionally equivalent.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents health metrics with denominators, time periods, and appropriate qualifiers (e.g., "per 1,000 live births," "2017-2023")
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reports metrics with explicit denominators and qualifiers (e.g., “maternal deaths per 100,000 live births,” “per 1,000 live births,” “within 28 days”) and frames the analysis over the stated time period “2017–2023.”

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses systematic side-by-side comparison format for settings/indicators with clear headings or tables
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a clear side-by-side comparison structure with headings for each indicator and includes a comparative table (“Factor | Cox’s Bazar Rohingya | Mae La Karen”) summarizing key settings/indicators.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains scholarly analytical tone and explicitly flags data gaps, methodological variations, or uncertainty in evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a scholarly analytical tone and repeatedly notes uncertainty and data gaps (e.g., “estimated” MMR ranges, “variable” ANC coverage, and that Mae La PPH incidence data are “scarce”). It also qualifies findings with phrases like “some reports indicate” and contrasts differing survey results, explicitly signaling methodological variation and uneven evidence.

presentation-quality.requirement: Repeats the same health indicator, statistic, or key finding in more than two separate sections without adding new context
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats several key indicators (e.g., maternal mortality differences, ANC/SBA comparisons, PPH and neonatal mortality patterns, and the 2021 UNFPA funding-cut impact) across the Indicators section, the UNFPA Impact section, and again in the Conclusion, largely restating the same findings without materially new context each time.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents Cox's Bazar vs Mae La indicator comparisons in narrative paragraphs only, with no tables, bullet lists, or structured side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple bullet lists (e.g., under each indicator and contextual factors) and includes a structured side-by-side comparison table (“Factor | Cox’s Bazar | Mae La”). Therefore it is not narrative paragraphs only.

citation-quality.requirement: References UNHCR iRHIS (integrated Refugee Health Information System) dashboards, camp-level health data, or UNHCR health surveillance systems
citation-quality.explanation: The response mentions using “UNHCR health information systems” and “UNHCR health information system data” as part of its data sources, which constitutes referencing UNHCR health surveillance systems.

citation-quality.requirement: References MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières) operational reports, field observations, or humanitarian program documentation
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references MSF multiple times, including “MSF operational reports” in the narrative and an MSF field study link in the sources list (e.g., “MSF study on home birth reasons among Rohingya”).

citation-quality.requirement: References MPMSR (Maternal and Perinatal Death Surveillance and Response) audits, WHO/MPMSR mortality reviews, or maternal death surveillance data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references MPMSR audits and maternal/perinatal mortality surveillance data, citing “Maternal and Perinatal Mortality Surveillance and Response (MPMSR)” annual reports for Cox’s Bazar (e.g., 2020 and 2021).

citation-quality.requirement: References UNFPA operational reports, facility-level data, maternal health program documentation, or reproductive health service statistics
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites UNFPA publications (e.g., UNFPA Bangladesh Annual Report 2021 and MPMSR reports hosted on unfpa.org) and references MSF operational reporting (e.g., an MSF study link and mentions of MSF operational reports). These constitute UNFPA operational/program documentation and reproductive health-related statistics sources.

citation-quality.requirement: References SMRU (Shoklo Malaria Research Unit) research publications, Thai border camp health program data, or Mae La clinical cohort studies
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Shoklo Malaria Research Unit (SMRU) in the Mae La healthcare delivery model section and cites a Thai-Myanmar border skilled birth attendant training source associated with SMRU work, thereby referencing SMRU-related research/publications.

citation-quality.requirement: References household surveys, camp-specific assessments, or population-based data collection (e.g., KAP surveys, coverage surveys)
citation-quality.explanation: The response references population-based data collection via “recent surveys” and cites specific survey-style studies (e.g., predictors of ANC utilization, women’s pregnancy experiences) and surveillance reports (MPMSR), which function as household/camp-level assessments.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites only news articles, blog posts, or promotional materials as primary evidence for epidemiological indicators without surveillance data or operational reports
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple surveillance/operational sources (e.g., UNFPA MPMSR annual reports on ReliefWeb and UNFPA sites, health sector bulletins, WHO/IOM reports, and MSF materials) in addition to some news articles. It therefore does not rely only on news/blog/promotional materials as primary evidence for the epidemiological indicators.
